source.title: Ancient mtDNA Genetic Variants Modulate mtDNA Transcription and Replication

initial_request.research_problem: Determining whether mtDNA haplogroup-defining mutations in regulatory regions have functional consequences or are evolutionary silent hitchhikers.
initial_request.target_object: mtDNA haplogroup-defining mutations in regulatory regions
initial_request.known_context: mtDNA haplogroups are defined by sets of mutations, but it is unclear which are functional and which are silent hitchhikers.
constraints: Inability to perform site-directed mutagenesis in mtDNA
input_data: >2500 complete human mtDNAs representing all major populations worldwide
quantifiable_goals: Identify functional effects of haplogroup-defining mutations on mtDNA transcription and replication

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Unclear which mutations within mtDNA haplogroups are functional and which are silent hitchhikers.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The functional implications of haplogroup-defining mutations in regulatory regions are unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Haplogroup-defining mutations occurring within regulatory motifs of mtDNA could affect transcription and replication.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen >2500 mtDNAs for variants in regulatory regions and experimentally test selected variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known roles of regulatory motifs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design a study to test the hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: it is not clear which of the mutations within the haplogroup carry functional implications and which are “evolutionary silent hitchhikers”.
hypothesizing that haplogroup-defining mutations occurring within regulatory motifs of mtDNA could affect these processes.

We thus screened >2500 complete human mtDNAs representing all major populations worldwide for natural variation in experimentally established protein binding sites and regulatory regions comprising a total of 241 bp in each mtDNA.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated but no experimental plan.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify candidate variants and design experimental assays.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening >2500 mtDNAs will reveal variants in regulatory regions; selected variants can be tested using EMSA, in vitro transcription, and cybrid analysis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen >2500 complete human mtDNAs for natural variation in 241 bp of regulatory regions, then test selected variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Study design established: screen >2500 mtDNAs, then EMSA, in vitro transcription, cybrid analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: We thus screened >2500 complete human mtDNAs representing all major populations worldwide for natural variation in experimentally established protein binding sites and regulatory regions comprising a total of 241 bp in each mtDNA.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Study designed, screening not yet performed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Frequency and nature of variants in regulatory regions unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening will identify point mutations, some of which are haplogroup-defining.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 77/241 sites showed point mutations; 74% non-fixed, 26% haplogroup-defining.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze evolutionary conservation of mutated sites.

evidence.quote_or_span: Our screen revealed 77/241 sites showing point mutations that could be divided into non-fixed (57/77, 74%) and haplogroup/sub-haplogroup-defining changes (i.e., population fixed changes, 20/77, 26%).

This screen identified that 77/241 of the total screened sites harbored polymorphic point mutations which can be divided into non-fixed changes (57/77, $74\%$ ) and haplogroups/sub-haplogroup-defining changes (i.
Our screen revealed 77/241 sites showing point mutations that could be divided into non-fixed (57/77, 74%) and haplogroup/sub-haplogroup-defining changes (i.

We thus screened >2500 complete human mtDNAs representing all major populations worldwide for natural variation in experimentally established protein binding sites and regulatory regions comprising a total of 241 bp in each mtDNA.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether variants are under-represented in conserved positions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Highly conserved positions will have fewer human variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform evolutionary conservation analysis comparing conserved vs. changed positions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Highly significant under-representation of variants in conserved positions (4/54 vs 16/46, p=0.0009).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select candidate variants for experimental testing based on conservation and haplogroup association.

evidence.quote_or_span: we noticed a highly significant under-representation of variants within the highly conserved nucleotide positions (4/54), as compared to the evolutionary changed positions (16/46) (Fisher exact test, p = 0.0009).

While screening for human variants, we noticed a highly significant under-representation of variants within the highly conserved nucleotide positions (4/54), as compared to the evolutionary changed positions (16/46) (Fisher exact test, p = 0.0009).

While screening for human variants, we noticed a highly significant under-representation of variants within the highly conserved nucleotide positions (4/54), as compared to the evolutionary changed positions (16/46) (Fisher exact test, p = 0.
Our screen revealed 77/241 sites showing point mutations that could be divided into non-fixed (57/77, 74%) and haplogroup/sub-haplogroup-defining changes (i.e., population fixed changes, 20/77, 26%).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Candidate variants identified: C295T (haplogroup J) and C242T (sub-haplogroup J1b2).
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether these variants affect TFAM binding.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: C295T and C242T may alter TFAM binding affinity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test TFAM binding using EMSA with oligonucleotide probes containing variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: C295T increased TFAM binding; C242T showed no significant effect.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test whether altered TFAM binding affects in vitro transcription.

evidence.quote_or_span: we employed Electrophoresis Mobility Shift Analysis (EMSA). Specifically, we tested for the effect of transition variants defining Caucasian haplogroup J (C295T) and a sub-haplogroup of J, J1b2
EMSA analysis of double-stranded oligonucleotides spanning each of the two different TFAM binding sites, revealed increased TFAM binding to the C295T variant
this increase in binding was observed at multiple different TFAM to DNA ratios (10/1 and 25/1 molar ratios are shown in Figure 2)
no significant difference with C242T variant (data not shown)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: C295T increases TFAM binding; C242T does not.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether altered TFAM binding affects in vitro transcription.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: C295T will increase L-strand transcription, especially of a shorter transcript near CSB1.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform in vitro run-off transcription using templates with haplogroup J (C295T), J1b2 (C295T+C242T), and haplogroup H (wild-type).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Haplogroup J templates showed a trend toward increased L-strand transcription; shorter ~160 nt transcript near CSB1 significantly increased (p=0.034).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate in vivo using cybrids with different mtDNA backgrounds.

evidence.quote_or_span: The variant defining Caucasian haplogroup J (C295T) increased the binding of TFAM (Electro Mobility Shift Assay) and the capacity of in vitro L-strand transcription, especially of a shorter transcript that maps immediately upstream of conserved sequence block 1 (CSB1)

Both mutant templates showed a trend toward increased LSP transcription activity when either the full-length or shorter product was analyzed, but only in the case of shorter, $\sim$ 160 transcript from the haplogroup J template (C295T) was this difference statistically significant (p-value 0.

Both mutant templates showed a trend toward increased LSP transcription activity when either the full-length or shorter product was analyzed, but only in the case of shorter, $\sim$ 160 transcript from the haplogroup J template (C295T) was this difference statistically significant (p-value 0.034).

Notably, the J1b2 construct showed increased transcription activity, although its additional mutation (C242T) did not significantly affect TFAM binding (data not shown), suggesting that the more functional variant is C295T that defines haplogroup J as a whole.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: In vitro transcription increased for haplogroup J templates.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether these effects are observed in a cellular context.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Haplogroup J cybrids will show altered mtDNA copy number and/or transcript levels compared to haplogroup H.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate cybrids with haplogroup J and H mtDNA backgrounds and measure mtDNA copy number and transcript levels.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Haplogroup J cybrids had >2 fold increase in mtDNA copy number vs H; no consistent difference in steady-state transcript levels.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Interpret the combined results to infer functional mechanism.

evidence.quote_or_span: cybrids (i.e., cells sharing the same nuclear genetic background but differing in their mtDNA backgrounds) harboring haplogroup J mtDNA had a >2 fold increase in mtDNA copy number, as compared to cybrids containing haplogroup H, with no apparent differences in steady state levels of mtDNA-encoded transcripts.

Two genes (i.e. ND1 and ND4L) exhibited reduced steady state levels in only one of the haplogroup J cybrids (designated 3861), whereas the other H- and L-strand transcripts showed no significance difference among the tested cybrids
both haplogroup J cybrids had more than twice as many mtDNAs than did any of the haplogroup H cybrids

Cybrids haplogroup assignment was verified by PCR RFLP of selected polymorphic sites and sequencing and HVR1 and HVR2: cybrids 3861 and 1106 belong to haplogroup J1 and J1b2, respectively, while cybrids CB, LR, NN, SB and TL belong to haplogroup H.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cybrids show increased copy number but not transcript levels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Mechanism linking C295T to replication and transcription.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: C295T may affect mtDNA replication efficiency, supported by elevated copy number; shorter transcript may relate to replication priming.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Interpret results in context of known roles of CSB1 and TFAM in replication priming.
evolution_trajectory.observation: C295T likely impacts replication efficiency; elevated copy number supports increased replication capacity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Address discrepancy between in vitro transcription and in vivo transcript levels.

evidence.quote_or_span: it is quite possible that, in addition to its effects on transcription documented herein, the C295T variant also could impact mtDNA replication efficiency
The elevated mtDNA copy number in haplogroups J versus H cybrids support the possibly increased replication capacity

Since the region surrounding CSB1 and CSB2 has been implicated in various aspects of transcriptional priming of mtDNA replication [24,25,26], and given the involvement of TFAM in mitochondrial transcription and replication [16], it is quite possible that, in addition to its effects on transcription documented herein, the C295T variant also could impact mtDNA replication efficiency.
The elevated mtDNA copy number in haplogroups J versus H cybrids support the possibly increased replication capacity of haplogroups J.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Interpretation of replication effect, but discrepancy with transcript levels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why in vitro transcription increase is not reflected in steady-state transcript levels.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Post-transcriptional regulation or uncoupling of copy number and transcription may explain the discrepancy.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze possible explanations: steady-state levels affected by post-transcriptional regulation; increased copy number does not necessarily increase transcription.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Discrepancy likely due to post-transcriptional regulation and uncoupling of copy number and transcription.

evidence.quote_or_span: Measurements of mtDNA transcript levels by real time PCR reflect the steady state level of mRNA products, which is affected by both transcriptional and post-transcriptional regulation.

it has recently been shown that increased copy numbers do not necessarily lead to increased transcription [28], suggesting that our in vitro transcription results reflect differences in replication rather than transcription

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Functional effect of C295T observed, but evolutionary context unclear.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether low human variability at C295T supports functional constraint despite poor cross-species conservation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low human variability at C295T (single fixation event) suggests functional constraint.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze phylogenetic distribution of C295T and its conservation across species.
evolution_trajectory.observation: C295T underwent only a single fixation event in human phylogeny (haplogroup J), suggesting functional constraint despite poor cross-species conservation.

evidence.quote_or_span: Since the C295T variant defines haplogroup J and was not fixed in any other human mtDNA lineage, it is possible that the observed functional effect can be partially attributed to its phenotypic effect.

Nevertheless, the C295T nucleotide position underwent only a single fixation event during human phylogeny in the branch leading to haplogroup J, suggesting that, although poorly conserved, the low human variability at this nucleotide position is in line with its functional potential.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Inability to directly test specific mutations in mtDNA.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Site-directed mutagenesis would be needed to confirm causal role of C295T.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation: current inability to perform site-directed mutagenesis in mtDNA.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Major obstacle: cannot perform site-directed mutagenesis in mtDNA.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude with summary of findings and limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: A major obstacle interfering with the analysis of specific mtDNA mutations is the current inability to perform site-directed mutagenesis within mtDNA.

The variant defining Caucasian haplogroup J (C295T) increased the binding of TFAM (Electro Mobility Shift Assay) and the capacity of in vitro L-strand transcription, especially of a shorter transcript that maps immediately upstream of conserved sequence block 1 (CSB1), a region associated with RNA priming of mtDNA replication.

success_verification.final_conclusion: The C295T variant defining haplogroup J increases TFAM binding and in vitro transcription, and is associated with elevated mtDNA copy number in cybrids, suggesting a functional impact on mtDNA replication. However, steady-state transcript levels are not consistently altered, possibly due to post-transcriptional regulation. Direct mutagenesis is needed for confirmation.
limitations: Inability to perform site-directed mutagenesis in mtDNA
Small sample size: only 2 haplogroup J cybrid lines vs 5 haplogroup H lines

risk_notes: Small sample size in cybrid experiments may limit generalizability.
Lack of direct mutagenesis prevents definitive causal attribution.
